matches.snippet: Title page: FINNEGANS WAKE
matches.reasoning: Finnegans is the direct English garbled form of Fionn mac Cumhaill; the title announces Finn's balladic wake.

matches.snippet: FINNEGANS WAKE - title reproduction
matches.reasoning: Repeated title form establishing Finnegan/Finn as central figure.

matches.snippet: Book opening: FINNEGANS WAKE
matches.reasoning: Title repeated at book opening, marking Finn as hero of the narrative.

matches.snippet: riverrun, past Eve and Adam's, from swerve of shore to bend of bay, brings us by a commodius vicus of recirculation back to Howth Castle and Environs.
matches.reasoning: Howth is explicitly the sleeping giant's head in Wake mythology; the recirculation motif echoes the cyclical fall-and-rise of the hero.

matches.snippet: The fall (bababadalgharaghtakamminarronnkonnbronntqnner-ronntuonnthunntrovarrhounawnskawntoohoohoordenenthurnuk!) of a once wallstrait oldparr
matches.reasoning: The thunderword represents Finnegan's/HCE's great fall, the cosmic collapse that is the mythic center of the work.

matches.snippet: The great fall of the offwall entailed at such short notice the pftjschute of Finnegan, erse solid man
matches.reasoning: Direct naming of Finnegan as the fallen figure; 'erse solid man' alludes to his Irish/earthy nature and corporeality.

matches.snippet: the humptyhillhead of humself prumptly sends an unquiring one well to the west
matches.reasoning: Humpty = Humpty Dumpty fused with the giant's humped head; hillhead suggests Howth or the sleeping giant's topography.

matches.matched_text: Bygmester  Finnegan,  of  the  Stuttering  Hand,  freemen's  maurer
matches.snippet: Bygmester Finnegan, of the Stuttering Hand, freemen's maurer, lived in the broadest way immarginable
matches.reasoning: 'Bygmester' = builder/master builder (Norwegian/Danish); Finnegan is explicitly identified as the builder/hod-carrier figure; 'freemen's maurer' = mason/bricklayer.

matches.matched_text: this  man  of  hod,  cement  and  edifices
matches.snippet: during mighty odd years this man of hod, cement and edifices in Toper's Thorp piled buildung supra buildung
matches.reasoning: 'man of hod' explicitly identifies Finnegan as the hod-carrier/bricklayer avatar; cement and edifices confirm his builder role.

matches.snippet: He addle liddle phifie Annie ugged the little craythur
matches.reasoning: 'liddle' puns on 'little' and echoes 'Lilith'; 'phifie' may play on 'wife' or 'fife'; fragmentary language suggests Finn's speech distortion or the stuttering epithet.

matches.matched_text: Mister  Finn,  you're  going  to  be  Mister  Finnagain!
matches.snippet: Hootch is for husbandman handling his hoe. Hohohoho, Mister Finn, you're going to be Mister Finnagain! Comeday morm and, O, you're vine!
matches.reasoning: 'Mister Finn' direct form; 'Finnagain' = Finn again, the resurrection pun that encodes the cyclical return of the hero.

matches.matched_text: Hahahaha,  Mister  Funn,  you're  going  to  be  fined  again!
matches.snippet: Sendday's eve and, ah, you're vinegar! Hahahaha, Mister Funn, you're going to be fined again!
matches.reasoning: 'Funn' variant spelling of Finn; 'fined' puns on 'Finn' + 'fine' (penalty); suggests Finn's transgression and legal consequences.

matches.matched_text: Macool,  Macool,  orra  whyi  deed  ye  diie
matches.snippet: Shize. I should shee! Macool, Macool, orra whyi deed ye diie. of a trying thirstay mournin.
matches.reasoning: Macool is the direct Anglicized form of Mac Cumhaill (son of Cumhall); the repeated invocation and funeral context evoke the balladic mourning for Finnegan.

matches.snippet: of a trying thirstay mournin. Sobs they sighdid at Fillagain's chrissormiss wake, all the hoolivans of the nation
matches.reasoning: 'Fillagain' = Finn + 'again' variant; 'chrissormiss wake' = Christmas mass wake; the funeral gathering mourns the fallen hero.

matches.matched_text: well,  Him  a  being  so  on  the  flounder  of  his  bulk  like  an  overgrown  babeling
matches.snippet: Well, Him a being so on the flounder of his bulk like an overgrown babeling, let wee peep, see, at Hom
matches.reasoning: 'bulk' and 'babeling' suggest the giant's immense recumbent body; 'floundering' alludes to his helpless state.

matches.snippet: Whase on the joint of a desh. Finfoefom the Fush. Whase be his baken head.
matches.reasoning: 'Finfoefom' echoes 'Finn' with Joycean wordplay; 'Fush' = Fish, possibly evoking the Salmon of Knowledge (Finn's mythic attribute); the fragmentary form obscures its mythological roots.

matches.matched_text: Finiche  !  Only  a  fadograph  of  a  yestern  scene.
matches.snippet: But, lo, as you would quaffoff his fraudstuff and sink teeth through that pyth of a flowerwhite bodey behold of him as behemoth for he is noewhemoe. Finiche ! Only a fadograph of a yestern scene.
matches.reasoning: 'Finiche' = Fin + 'iche' (suffix); the word fades/dies ('Finiche') as the corpse dissolves into memory; the photograph captures the past hero.

matches.snippet: Almost rubicund Salmosalar, ancient fromout the ages of the Agapemonides, he is smolten in our mist
matches.reasoning: 'Salmosalar' = Salmon of Knowledge (Irish: an Bradán Feasa); the mythic fish is Finn's wisdom-bestowing attribute; here it merges with the sleeping giant.

matches.snippet: Yet may we not see still the brontoichthyan form outlined aslumbered, even in our own nighttime by the sedge
matches.reasoning: 'Brontoichthyan' = bronto (giant) + ichthyan (fish); fuses the giant and the salmon, two Finn avatars, in one recumbent form.

matches.matched_text: His  clay  feet,  swarded  in  verdigrass,  stick  up  starck  where  he  last  fellonem
matches.snippet: The cranic head on him, caster of his reasons, peer yuthner in yondmist. Whooth. His clay feet, swarded in verdigrass, stick up starck where he last fellonem
matches.reasoning: The feet sticking up evoke the sleeping giant's recumbent body as Dublin landscape; 'clay feet' suggest earth-bound mortality.

matches.snippet: Underwetter! Goat strip Finnlambs! This is jinnies rinning away to their ousterlists dowan a bunkersheels.
matches.reasoning: 'Finnlambs' = Finn + lambs; fragmented form embedded in warfare narrative; the hero's name is scattered through battle descriptions.
